chapters.title: The Mirror and the Splinters
chapters.text:
Now then! Let us begin. When we have got to the end of the story we shall know more than we do now. Once there was a wicked hobgoblin, one of the very worst — for he was the devil himself. One day he made a mirror that had the power of making everything good and beautiful reflected in it shrink almost to nothing, while everything worthless and ugly became bold and glaring.
The goblin's pupils ran about with the mirror, and at last they wished to fly up to heaven itself, to mock the angels. But the higher they flew, the more the mirror grinned and shook — until it slipped from their hands, fell to the earth, and broke into a hundred million million pieces.
Some pieces were no bigger than a grain of sand, and these blew about the wide world. When a splinter lodged in a person's eye, they saw everything crookedly, or saw only what was ugly. And when one pierced a heart, that was most dreadful of all — for the heart turned to a lump of ice.

chapters.title: A Little Boy and a Little Girl
chapters.text:
High over the city, between two attic windows, Gerda and Kay kept a little garden of roses in wooden boxes. They were not brother and sister, but they loved one another as dearly as if they were.
Kay: “The roses are open, Gerda! Summer has truly come.”
Gerda: “Let us promise to stay friends forever, Kay — no matter what happens.”
Kay: “Forever. I promise.”
In the evenings Grandmother would gather them close and tell them stories. One night, as snow began to fall, she spoke of the Snow Queen.
Grandmother: “See the snowflakes swarming? Their queen flies where they crowd thickest.”
Kay: “Let her come! I will set her on the warm stove and she will melt!”
Grandmother: “Hush, child. Do not tempt the winter.”
Two of those splinters found Kay that winter — one pricked his heart, and one flew into his eye. At once his heart grew cold, and everything lovely looked hateful to him.
Gerda: “Kay, why are you tearing the roses? You are frightening me.”
Kay: “They are ugly, eaten by worms. Look — a snowflake is far more perfect than any flower.”
Gerda: “That is not you speaking. Where are you going with your sled?”
Kay: “To the square, where the clever boys tie their sleds to the passing carts. Leave me be.”
The Snow Queen: “Are you cold, little one? Come, creep under my bearskin cloak.”
Kay: “Your kiss is colder than ice... I cannot feel the cold at all now...”
The Snow Queen: “One more kiss, and you will forget little Gerda entirely. Now, sleep.”
And the sleigh rose into the storm and was gone. All that winter Kay did not return. The people said he must have fallen through the river ice and drowned. But Gerda did not believe it.

chapters.title: The River and the Enchanted Garden
chapters.text:
When spring came, Gerda put on her new red shoes and went down to the river to ask if it had taken Kay away.
Gerda: “River, did you take Kay? I will give you my new red shoes if you give him back.”
Gerda: “You will not take them? Then he is not here. I will keep searching.”
A little boat carried Gerda downstream, past green banks, until she came to a cottage ringed with flowers, where an old woman who understood magic longed for a little daughter.
The Garden Woman: “Stay with me, sweet child. It is always summer in my garden.”
The Garden Woman: “I will comb your hair, and you will forget everything that troubles you.”
Gerda: “How warm it is here... what was it I was looking for...?”
The old woman hid every rose beneath the earth, so Gerda would not remember Kay. But one day Gerda saw a rose painted on the woman's hat — and wept, and her tears woke the buried roses, and all her memory returned.
The roses knew Kay was not among the dead, and Gerda's hope blazed up. She ran to the other flowers and asked each one, 'Do you know where Kay is?' But every flower knew only its own dream: the tiger-lily sang of a widow on a burning pyre; the convolvulus of a castle on a cliff; the little snowdrop of two children on a swing. None of them had ever heard of Kay.
Gerda: “You dream your own dreams, and none of them is Kay. I have wasted the whole summer here — I must go, at once!”
Gerda ran to the garden gate. It was no longer summer beyond the wall — it was cold, late autumn, and she had lost so many days. On her bare feet she hurried away, and the whole world seemed grey and old.

chapters.title: The Crow, the Prince and the Princess
chapters.text:
It was autumn now. On the road a tame crow met Gerda and cocked its head at her sorrow.
The Crow: “Caw! A princess here wished for a husband who could truly talk — and a clever boy won her hand. Might he be your Kay?”
Gerda: “Oh, please — take me to him. I must see his face.”
The princess, said the crow, was so clever she had read every newspaper in the world. She wished for a husband who could answer when spoken to — not merely stand and look grand. Suitors came in crowds, but their tongues failed them in the shining halls; only one little fellow walked in with bright eyes and no fear at all, and spoke as easily as the crows themselves. Him the princess chose.
By night the crow led Gerda up the back stairs and through the sleeping palace, where dream-shadows swept past like hunters and horses on the walls.
The Prince: “You woke me, little one. But no — I am not the Kay you seek.”
The Princess: “Poor child. Take warm boots, a muff, and our golden coach. May they carry you to him.”
Gerda: “Thank you — thank you both. I will never forget your kindness.”
So Gerda climbed into the coach of pure gold, and the prince and princess and the kind crow waved her away. The wheels rolled on through the dark, cold forest, and their light shone out like a beacon — which the robbers, watching from the trees, could not resist.

chapters.title: The Little Robber Girl
chapters.text:
In a dark forest robbers fell upon the golden coach. A wild little robber girl, fierce as she was lonely, decided to keep Gerda for a playmate.
The Robber Mother: “Fresh little thing! Shall we roast her, or keep her? Ha!”
The Robber Girl: “She is mine. She will sleep beside me, and no one will harm her — or I will bite them.”
Gerda: “I only want to find Kay. Please — have you seen him?”
The robber girl kept a whole menagerie in the smoky hall: a hundred wood-pigeons cooing overhead, and a reindeer tethered by a bright ring of copper. She was a head taller than Gerda, with strong hands, and she slept each night with an arm about Gerda's neck and a knife close by. 'They shall not kill you,' she said, 'unless I grow angry with you myself.'
That night the wood-pigeons in the robber girl's menagerie cooed a secret they had seen.
Bae the Reindeer: “*The pigeons coo: we saw Kay! The Snow Queen bore him north, toward Lapland, to her palace of ice.*”
The Robber Girl: “Then take my reindeer — he was born up there and knows the way. Go on. Find your friend.”
Gerda: “You are kinder than you pretend to be. Thank you.”

chapters.title: Lapland and Finland
chapters.text:
On and on the reindeer ran, over the great empty snows, and above them the northern lights burned green and blue, a cold fire in the sky. They ran until they came to a little smoke-blackened hut in Lapland, where an old woman was frying fish by the light of a train-oil lamp.
The Lapland Woman: “You have come far, child. I will write to the Finland woman on a dried cod — she is wiser than I.”
Gerda: “Then I will go on. Whatever it takes, I will reach him.”
The reindeer ran on to the smoky hut of the Finland woman, who read the cod-letter three times until she knew it by heart, and then boiled it for soup.
Bae the Reindeer: “*The reindeer whispers: give her the strength of twelve men, so she may defeat the Snow Queen.*”
The Finland Woman: “I can give her no greater power than she already has. Do you not see? Her power is her innocent, loving heart.”
Gerda: “Then set me down at the gate. My heart will carry me the rest of the way.”
The reindeer set Gerda down by the great red-berry bush at the edge of the Queen's land, and could go no further. Snowflake-sentinels whirled up to stop her — but Gerda breathed a prayer, and her warm breath became little angels who blunted the cold and cleared her path.

chapters.title: The Snow Queen's Palace
chapters.text:
The palace walls were of driven snow, its windows and doors of cutting wind, lit only by the northern lights. Its halls were vast, and empty, and still. The Snow Queen herself had flown far south, to breathe frost on the warm countries.
In the middle of the endless frozen lake sat Kay — almost black with cold, yet feeling nothing — pushing flat shards of ice this way and that. He was trying to spell a single word: eternity.
Gerda: “Kay! Dear Kay! I have found you at last!”
Kay: “...Who is there? The pieces... I must make them spell the word...”
Gerda threw her arms around him and wept, and her hot tears fell on his chest and sank to his heart. They melted the ice and burned away the splinter within. Kay burst into tears of his own, and wept out the glass from his eye. 'Gerda!' he cried — and knew her.
They laughed and danced for joy — and the very shards of ice danced with them, and when they grew tired they lay down and spelled, all by themselves, the word Kay had sought: eternity. Now — help him finish it.
Kay was free. Hand in hand the two friends walked out of the palace of ice, and wherever they stepped the cutting wind fell still. The reindeer was waiting, and carried them back the whole long way — past the Finland woman and the Lapland woman, through the robber-wood now turning green, where the little robber girl, grown taller, rode out to greet them.
And so they came home at last — through the same gate, up the same stair. And when they stepped into the old room, everything stood as before; but they saw that they had grown up, and were grown-up people now, and yet children still, children at heart. It was summer — warm, glorious summer.

chapters.title: Home, and Summer Again
chapters.text: Hand in hand, Gerda and Kay journeyed home through Finland, Lapland and the robber wood, and everywhere the spring was breaking through. When they reached the city they were grown tall, yet children still at heart. The roses bloomed on the rooftop, and Grandmother read aloud: 'Except ye become as little children, ye shall not enter the kingdom of heaven.' And it was summer — warm, glorious summer.
